summary: Martin Fowler shares his enthusiasm for strategic hobby board games, particularly Eurogames, which are distinct from mass-market games like Monopoly. He maintains an active blog on BoardGameGeek called 'Martin's Seventh Decade' where he documents his gaming experiences. The page serves as an index to his board game writings, covering play reports, game reviews, and analytical pieces about game design concepts. His interests center on economic and route-building games like Brass, Age of Steam, and Power Grid, with recent exploration into the 18xx genre.
key_points: Strategic hobby games are fundamentally different from mass-market games - Fowler hasn't played Monopoly in decades and has no interest in returning to it
BoardGameGeek serves as the primary platform for his gaming writing, though it lacks good indexing capabilities
His gaming analysis extends beyond reviews to conceptual pieces on game design (tightness, victory points, player interaction, theme vs setting)
Economic and route-building games dominate his preferences, particularly the Brass and Age of Steam families
He's recently been exploring 18xx games after having a 'love-hate relationship' with the genre
money_quotes: It's been many decades since I've played Monopoly, and I have no interest in ever playing it again.
The kinds of board games I play are what I call strategic hobby games. These are a long distance from the mass market board games that you might find in most stores.
Brass is a hugely successful family of games, and if forced to pick a favorite game, it's the one I'd pick.
I've long had a love-hate relationship with 18xx games. There's so much that appeals to me about an economic game with a strong tie to history.
Victory points are almost omnipresent in board gaming, yet often they encourage games to become unfocused.
key_insight: Board gaming as a hobby involves strategic depth and thoughtful design analysis that mass-market games simply cannot provide.
